ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product entry; no specific entry for deep-fried baby squid. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers squid and cuttlefish as a category; no specific deep-fried preparation sub-code available. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for fried baby squid dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried baby sotong at a Singapore hawker centre is approximately 100g, comprising several whole deep-fried baby squid pieces with a light batter coating.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Baby Sotong — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Baby Sotong (CR ID: F03060008). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical source. Saturated fat, trans fat, and sugar sub-fractions are adapted from USDA FoodData Central analogous deep-fried squid entries where HPB does not report these sub-fractions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried baby sotong. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous battered and deep-fried seafood products with wheat-flour batter coating from the University of Sydney GI Database. The batter contributes the primary glycaemic load; squid flesh itself has negligible carbohydrate. Carbohydrate per serving 12.3g × GI 55 / 100 = GL 6.8 per 100g serving. GI 55 is classified as low by the University of Sydney GI Database (GI ≤ 55 is low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 741 mg per 100g — exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit fried baby sotong due to its critically high sodium content of 741 mg/100g; if consumed, restrict to a very small portion (≤50g) and avoid additional salty condiments or dipping sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (12.3g/100g) with high protein (30.2g/100g) moderates glycaemic response, but deep-frying adds significant fat and calories that can worsen insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume fried baby sotong in small portions occasionally, as the high protein content (30.2g/100g) slows gastric emptying and attenuates glucose spikes, but the deep-fried preparation adds excess saturated fat and calories that impair long-term insulin sensitivity; grilled or steamed squid is strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (27.5g/100g), saturated fat (~4.8g/100g), trans fat (0.3g/100g), and high sodium (741mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid fried baby sotong due to its combination of high sodium (741mg/100g), elevated total fat (27.5g/100g), saturated fat, and trans fat from deep-frying, all of which contribute to arterial stiffness, dyslipidaemia, and elevated blood pressure; steamed or grilled squid is a far safer alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and trans fat that directly promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid fried baby sotong as deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs and trans fat (0.3g/100g) that exacerbate synovial inflammation; steamed or grilled squid retains the anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefit without the inflammatory frying by-products.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried baby sotong is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of whole baby squid (sotong) that are coated in a light seasoned batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is commonly found at hawker centres, food courts, and zi char stalls across Singapore. The squid flesh is tender and slightly chewy inside, while the batter exterior is crisp and savoury. It is typically served with chilli sauce or mayonnaise as a dipping condiment. Nutritionally, fried baby sotong is energy-dense at 418 kcal per 100g, with high protein (30.2g/100g) from the squid and high fat (27.5g/100g) from the deep-frying process. Sodium is critically elevated at 741mg/100g due to seasoning, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The trans fat content (approximately 0.3g/100g) from high-temperature frying is a concern for both cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. Despite the moderate carbohydrate content (12.3g/100g), the high protein slows gastric emptying, giving it a low glycaemic impact (GI 55). Calcium (65mg/100g) and iron (2.9mg/100g) are notable micronutrients. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker contexts. For health-conscious consumers, grilled or steamed squid is strongly preferred over the deep-fried version.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried baby sotong?
Is fried squid high in sodium?
Is fried baby sotong suitable for hypertension?
Is fried sotong high in fat?
Can I eat fried squid if I have heart disease?
What is the GI of fried squid?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F03060008) with confidence 0.95. The record is canonical, reviewed, and provenance is HPB. Saturated fat and trans fat values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous deep-fried squid entries to supplement HPB data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for battered deep-fried seafood.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for fried baby sotong not reported in HPB Foodo database; USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact match for this Singapore preparation. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and water values are not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for this food item. saturated_fat_g (4.8g) and trans_fat_g (0.3g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for deep-fried squid (analogous entries) as HPB does not report these sub-fractions; documented as adapted source. sugar_g (0.5g) estimated from USDA analogous fried squid entries as HPB does not report sugar separately. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — squid flesh and standard batter contain negligible dietary fibre. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate for battered deep-fried seafood from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for fried baby sotong exists in published literature as of 2026. GI 55 is classified as low by the University of Sydney GI Database (GI ≤ 55 is low).
